title: Cito Pede Preterit Aetas - A Philosophical Dissertation
content:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Gillianï؟½s dead, God rest her bier,
آ آ آ آ How I loved her many years syne;
آ آ آ آ Marionï؟½s married, but I sit here,
آ آ آ آ Alive and merry at three-score year,
آ آ آ آ Dipping my nose in Gascoigne wine.ï؟½
آ آ آ آ - Wambaï؟½s Song, Thackeray.


آ آ آ آ A mellower light doth Sol afford,
آ آ آ آ His meridian glare has passï؟½d,
آ آ آ آ And the trees on the broad and sloping sward
آ آ آ آ Their lengthï؟½ning shadows cast.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Time flies.ï؟½ The current will be no joke,
آ آ آ آ If swollen by recent rain,
آ آ آ آ To cross in the dark, so Iï؟½ll have a smoke,
آ آ آ آ And then Iï؟½ll be off again.

آ آ آ آ Whatï؟½s up, old horse? Your ears you prick,
آ آ آ آ And your eager eyeballs glisten;
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Tis the wild dogï؟½s note in the tea-tree thick,
آ آ آ آ By the river, to which you listen.
آ آ آ آ With head erect and tail flung out,
آ آ آ آ For a gallop you seem to beg,
آ آ آ آ But I feel the qualm of a chilling doubt,
آ آ آ آ As I glance at your favï؟½rite leg.

آ آ آ آ Let the dingo rest, ï؟½tis all for the best;
آ آ آ آ In this world thereï؟½s room enough
آ آ آ آ For him and you and me and the rest,
آ آ آ آ And the country is awful rough.
آ آ آ آ Weï؟½ve had our gallop in days of yore,
آ آ آ آ Now down the hill we must run;
آ آ آ آ Yet at times we long for one gallop more,
آ آ آ آ Although it were only one.

آ آ آ آ Did our spirits quail at a new four-rail,
آ آ آ آ Could a ï؟½doubleï؟½ double-bank us,
آ آ آ آ Ere nerve and sinew began to fail
آ آ آ آ In the consulship of Plancus?
آ آ آ آ When our blood ran rapidly, and when
آ آ آ آ Our bones were pliant and limber,
آ آ آ آ Could we stand a merry cross-counter then,
آ آ آ آ A slogging fall over timber?

آ آ آ آ Arcades ambo! Duffers both,
آ آ آ آ In our best of days, alas!
آ آ آ آ (I tell the truth, though to tell it loth)
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Tis time we were gone to grass;
آ آ آ آ The young leaves shoot, the sere leaves fall,
آ آ آ آ And the old gives way to the new,
آ آ آ آ While the preacher cries, ï؟½ï؟½tis vanity all,
آ آ آ آ And vexation of spirit, too.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ Now over my head the vapours curl
آ آ آ آ From the bowl of the soothing clay,
آ آ آ آ In the misty forms that eddy and whirl
آ آ آ آ My thoughts are flitting away;
آ آ آ آ Yes, the preacherï؟½s right, ï؟½tis vanity all,
آ آ آ آ But the sweeping rebuke he showers
آ آ آ آ On vanities all may heaviest fall
آ آ آ آ On vanities worse than ours.

آ آ آ آ We have no wish to exaggerate
آ آ آ آ The worth of the sports we prize,
آ آ آ آ Some toil for their Church, and some for their State,
آ آ آ آ And some for their merchandise;
آ آ آ آ Some traffic and trade in the cityï؟½s mart,
آ آ آ آ Some travel by land and sea,
آ آ آ آ Some follow science, some cleave to art,
آ آ آ آ And some to scandal and tea;

آ آ آ آ And some for their country and their queen
آ آ آ آ Would fight, if the chance they had,
آ آ آ آ Good sooth, ï؟½twere a sorry world, I ween,
آ آ آ آ If we all went galloping mad;
آ آ آ آ Yet if once we efface the joys of the chase
آ آ آ آ From the land, and outroot the Stud,
آ آ آ آ Good-bye to the anglo-saxon race!
آ آ آ آ Farewell to the norman blood!

آ آ آ آ Where the burn runs down to the uplands brown,
آ آ آ آ From the heights of the snow-clad range,
آ آ آ آ What anodyne drawn from the stifling town
آ آ آ آ Can be reckonï؟½d a fair exchange
آ آ آ آ For the stalkerï؟½s stride, on the mountain side,
آ آ آ آ In the bracing northern weather,
آ آ آ آ To the slopes where couch, in their antlerï؟½d pride,
آ آ آ آ The deer on the perfumï؟½d heather?

آ آ آ آ Oh! the vigour with which the air is rife!
آ آ آ آ The spirit of joyous motion;
آ آ آ آ The fever, the fulness of animal life,
آ آ آ آ Can be drainï؟½d from no earthly potion!
آ آ آ آ The lungs with the living gas grow light,
آ آ آ آ And the limbs feel the strength of ten,
آ آ آ آ While the chest expands with its maddï؟½ning might,
آ آ آ آ Godï؟½s glorious oxygen.

آ آ آ آ Thus the measurï؟½d stroke, on elastic sward,
آ آ آ آ Of the steed three parts extended
آ آ آ آ , Hard held, the breath of his nostrils broad,
آ آ آ آ With the golden ether blended;
آ آ آ آ Then the leap, the rise from the springy turf,
آ آ آ آ The rush through the buoyant air,
آ آ آ آ And the light shock landing, the veriest serf
آ آ آ آ Is an emperor then and there!

آ آ آ آ Such scenes! sensation and sound and sight!
آ آ آ آ To some undiscoverï؟½d shore
آ آ آ آ On the current of Timeï؟½s remorseless flight
آ آ آ آ Have they swept to return no more?
آ آ آ آ While, like phantoms bright of the feverï؟½d night,
آ آ آ آ That have vexï؟½d our slumbers of yore,
آ آ آ آ You follow us still in your ghostly might,
آ آ آ آ Dead days that have gone before.

آ آ آ آ Vain dreams, again and again re-told,
آ آ آ آ Must you crowd on the weary brain,
آ آ آ آ Till the fingers are cold that entwinï؟½d of old
آ آ آ آ Round foil and trigger and rein,
آ آ آ آ Till stayï؟½d for aye are the roving feet,
آ آ آ آ Till the restless hands are quiet,
آ آ آ آ Till the stubborn heart has forgotten to beat,
آ آ آ آ Till the hot blood has ceasï؟½d to riot?

آ آ آ آ In Exeter Hall the saint may chide,
آ آ آ آ The sinner may scoff outright,
آ آ آ آ The Bacchanal steepï؟½d in the flagonï؟½s tide,
آ آ آ آ Or the sensual Sybarite;
آ آ آ آ But Nolanï؟½s name will flourish in fame,
آ آ آ آ When our galloping days are past,
آ آ آ آ When we go to the place from whence we came,
آ آ آ آ Perchance to find rest at last.

آ آ آ آ Thy riddles grow dark, oh! drifting cloud,
آ آ آ آ And thy misty shapes grow drear,
آ آ آ آ Thou hangï؟½st in the air like a shadowy shroud,
آ آ آ آ But I am of lighter cheer;
آ آ آ آ Though our future lot is a sable blot,
آ آ آ آ Though the wise ones of earth will blame us,
آ آ آ آ Though our saddles will rot, and our rides be forgot,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Dum Vivimus, Vivamus!ï؟½
meaning: About: Adam Lindsay Gordon was an Australian poet, jockey and politician.
